skill.name: frontend-dev-guidelines
skill.description: Frontend development guidance for building Next.js, React 19, TypeScript, shadcn/ui, Tailwind CSS, Supabase, forms, routing, loading states, and performance patterns.
skill.summary: Guides modern frontend implementation with Next.js, React, TypeScript, shadcn/ui, and performance-focused patterns.
skill.author: DojoCodingLabs

security_audit.summary: Static analysis found many high-risk patterns, but review shows they are markdown examples, inline code formatting, imports, and local API examples rather than executable skill behavior. No prompt injection, credential exfiltration, malicious command execution, or hidden runtime code was found in reviewed files. The main residual risk is that simplified frontend examples may need project-specific security checks before use.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backticks Misclassified as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The external command detections are markdown code fences, inline identifiers, or a product slash command reference. They are not shell execution APIs and do not run commands from the skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed locations are documentation text or TypeScript import examples, not Ruby backticks or shell invocation. The static pattern is triggered by markdown syntax.

low_findings.title: Dynamic Imports Are Lazy Loading Examples
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: Dynamic import expressions appear inside React lazy loading examples. They are ordinary frontend code-splitting guidance and do not load remote or attacker-controlled modules.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The imports reference local component paths in markdown examples. No evidence was found of remote module loading or runtime execution by the skill.

low_findings.title: Relative Imports Misclassified as Path Traversal
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: Path traversal detections are TypeScript relative import examples such as feature-local imports. They are not filesystem reads, writes, deletes, or path joins using user input.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed lines are static import paths in documentation examples. There is no filesystem API call and no user-controlled path construction.

low_findings.title: Network Calls Are Local API Client Examples
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE with minor caution: The network detections are frontend examples for fetch and a shared axios apiClient. They do not send secrets to external domains, but generated applications should still review authentication and error handling.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The examples use relative API paths or a shared client description. No evidence was found of hardcoded external endpoints, credential collection, or exfiltration.

low_findings.title: Weak Crypto Alerts Are Textual Matches
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: Weak cryptography alerts align with headings, TypeScript patterns, or descriptive text, not crypto functions. No hash, cipher, or password storage implementation was found at the cited reviewed locations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The lines are ordinary documentation or React and TypeScript wording. The scanner appears to match short tokens, not real cryptographic usage.

low_findings.title: Simplified Mutation Examples Need Project Security Review
low_findings.description: NEEDS_REVIEW: Some examples show form data insertion and API route creation without surrounding authorization, validation, or rate limiting context. This is common in short documentation examples, but users should add security checks in real applications.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The examples are intentionally concise, so missing security context may be illustrative rather than unsafe intent. The concern is moderate because copied patterns could be deployed without required checks.

content.value_statement: Modern frontend projects often need consistent patterns across routing, data fetching, forms, styling, and loading states. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code practical guidance for Next.js and React implementation.
actual_capabilities: Guides Next.js App Router page and layout creation.
Explains Server Component and Client Component usage choices.
Provides form patterns using React Hook Form and Zod.
Documents loading, error, Suspense, and lazy loading patterns.
Covers TypeScript props, imports, file organization, and performance patterns.
Shows Supabase and API client data fetching examples.
limitations: It is an instructional skill, not an executable frontend framework.
Examples may need adaptation for projects that do not use Next.js or React.
Security, authorization, and validation details must be added for production code.
Some resource files mix shadcn, MUI, TanStack Router, and Supabase conventions.

use_cases.title: Create a Product Page
use_cases.description: Use the skill to plan a Next.js page with Server Components, metadata, loading states, and shadcn/ui structure.

use_cases.title: Standardize Team Components
use_cases.description: Use the guidelines to align component structure, TypeScript props, imports, forms, and error state patterns.

use_cases.title: Improve Frontend Performance
use_cases.description: Apply lazy loading, Suspense boundaries, memoization, and query caching guidance to reduce unnecessary client work.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Basic Page
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the frontend-dev-guidelines skill to create a Next.js App Router page for a simple dashboard. Include loading and error state files.

prompt_templates.title: Build a Typed Form
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the skill to design a React Hook Form workflow with Zod validation, shadcn/ui fields, and a Server Action mutation.

prompt_templates.title: Refactor Component Boundaries
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this React feature and split it into Server Components and Client Components using the skill guidelines.

prompt_templates.title: Optimize a Data Heavy View
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the skill to audit a data-heavy frontend view for Suspense boundaries, query caching, lazy loading, and TypeScript clarity.

output_examples.input: Create a dashboard page using the skill.
output_examples.output: The response outlines the page route, server data fetching approach, loading view, error view, UI components, and metadata needs.

output_examples.input: Review a form implementation for this stack.
output: Suggests React Hook Form and Zod structure.
Calls out missing submission and error states.

output_examples.input: Improve performance for a large table view.
output_examples.output: The response recommends lazy loading heavy components, adding Suspense boundaries, memoizing expensive transforms, and using query caching.

best_practices: Use Server Components by default and add Client Components only for interactivity.
Pair every data-heavy route with clear loading, error, and empty states.
Adapt examples to your authentication, validation, and deployment requirements.
anti_patterns: Do not add client-side rendering to every component by habit.
Do not copy simplified API examples into production without authorization checks.
Do not mix routing, styling, and state patterns without a project-level standard.

faq.question: What stack does this skill target?
faq.answer: It targets Next.js, React 19, TypeScript, shadcn/ui, Tailwind CSS, React Hook Form, Zod, and Supabase patterns.

faq.question: Can Codex use this skill?
faq.answer: Yes. The marketplace metadata lists Codex, Claude, and Claude Code as supported tools.

faq.question: Does the skill execute code?
faq.answer: No. The reviewed files are markdown guidance and examples. No executable scripts were found.

faq.question: Is it only for new projects?
faq.answer: No. It can guide new features, refactors, reviews, and performance improvements in existing frontend projects.

faq.question: Does it replace a security review?
faq.answer: No. Production API routes, Server Actions, and forms still need authorization, validation, and abuse controls.

faq.question: Why are there risk factors in the report?
faq.answer: The files contain example imports, fetch calls, and markdown code. These are documented as low-risk scanner matches.
